source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

The dog barked. Once. Then again. Silence. The neighbor's light came on — third window from the left, second floor. Off after four seconds. The dog did not bark again. Marcus wrote this down. He had been writing things down for six weeks. His notebook had 214 entries. None of them explained anything. But he kept writing.

Seven researchers. One hypothesis. Four years of fieldwork. The data did not cooperate. Kowalski wanted to publish anyway. The others said no. They did not say no loudly. They said it with silences, with rescheduled meetings, with emails that began "Just circling back." Kowalski understood. She filed the dataset under a folder called "misc" and closed her laptop. Nobody discussed it again. The grant ended in March. Three of the seven found new positions. This is not a story about failure. It is not a story about success either. The folder is still there.

The form required three signatures. It got two. The third person, Henderson, was in Lisbon at a supply chain conference. This mattered enormously. Or not at all. The files sat in the inbox from February until June. Nobody escalated. The word "escalate" never appeared in any email. "Flagging" appeared seven times. "Circling back" appeared eleven times. The files were eventually approved by someone named D. Marsh, whose title nobody in the office knew. Henderson returned from Lisbon. She was not told about the files. She never asked.

Room 7B had seventeen chairs. The meeting used four. The agenda had nine items. They covered two. The third item — funding — was tabled. Nobody moved to untable it. Priya had prepared a forty-slide deck for item three. She did not open her laptop. Afterward, she stood in the parking lot for eleven minutes. She counted the minutes. She does not know why she counted them. The deck is still in her downloads folder. Item three remains tabled.

The elevator in Building C had been out of service since Tuesday. There were eleven flights of stairs. Benedikta climbed them twice a day. She did not complain. Nobody on her floor complained. Complaining was not something they did. On Thursday, someone taped a note to the elevator door. The note said: "We apologize for the inconvenience." It was signed: "Management." Nobody knew who Management was. The elevator was fixed on a Saturday, when nobody was in the office. By Monday, four people had forgotten it was ever broken. Benedikta had not forgotten. She did not say so.

The report was forty-three pages. Marcus read eleven. The executive summary was two pages. He read neither. The report was due Thursday. It was submitted Thursday at 11:58 PM. No one commented on the time. No one commented on the report. It appeared in the shared drive under a folder called "Final_FINAL_v3." There were four earlier versions. None of them were labeled "Final." The report recommended discontinuing the pilot program. The pilot program continued. Marcus was not asked why. He did not volunteer an explanation. In April, someone requested the report again. Marcus sent them "Final_FINAL_v3." It was the same file. Nobody noticed. Or they noticed and said nothing. Both are possible.

The committee met on the second and fourth Tuesdays. In March, the second Tuesday fell on a holiday. The committee did not meet. There was no provision for this. The bylaws used the word "convene" fourteen times. They did not use the word "reschedule." Fowler sent an email asking what should happen. She received three replies. Two said to wait. One said nothing, which is to say it contained only her original message quoted in full, with no added text. The matter was resolved by not being resolved. In April, the committee convened on the second Tuesday. Nobody mentioned March. The minutes from April listed attendance as "quorum present." Fowler checked: four people attended. Quorum required five. She wrote this in a notebook. The notebook is in her desk. She has not shown it to anyone.

The survey had twenty-three questions. Eleven were required. The remaining twelve were labeled "optional," though the form would not submit without them. Nobody reported this. The survey was about employee satisfaction. It was administered in November. The results were summarized in a document titled "Findings." The document had no findings. It had seven bullet points, each beginning with the phrase "Employees indicated." What employees indicated was not specified. The survey's final question asked: "Do you feel heard?" Sixty-one percent answered yes. The document cited this as evidence of progress. Cressida had answered no. She was not asked to elaborate. The form did not have an elaboration field. She thought about adding one. She did not add one. The document was shared at the all-hands meeting. Nobody asked about the thirty-nine percent.

The training was mandatory. It was two hours long. It covered topics including but not limited to: active listening, constructive feedback, and conflict avoidance. The slides had forty-seven bullet points. Seven of them contained the word "synergy." Participants were asked to rate the training on a scale of one to five. The average score was 3.1. Nobody rated it a one. Nobody rated it a five. Vásquez rated it a two. She had attended the same training in 2019. The content was identical. She did not mention this on the feedback form. The feedback form did not ask. Afterward, her manager said: "Wasn't that useful?" Vásquez said: "Yes." This was not true. Her manager did not ask a follow-up question. There was no follow-up question.

The memo had three action items. The first was to schedule a follow-up meeting. The second was to circulate the revised budget by Friday. The third was to "ensure alignment across all relevant stakeholders," which was not an action item. No meeting was scheduled. The budget was circulated on Friday at 11:47 PM. Petrov did not open it. She was listed as the primary contact. This was noted in the subject line: "ATTN: Petrov — Budget (Final)." She had not been told she was the primary contact. She found out when someone replied-all asking her to explain the variance on line seventeen. She did not know what line seventeen was. The document had twenty-two lines. She counted. Line seventeen was blank. She replied that she would look into it. She has not looked into it. That was six weeks ago. The reply chain now has fourteen messages. Petrov is CC'd on all of them. She has read none of them.

The form required a signature. The form was digital. There was a signature field. It accepted typed names. Okonkwo typed her name. The form said her signature was invalid. There were no instructions. She typed her name again. The form accepted it. The two entries were identical. She could not explain why the second one worked. Nobody asked her to explain. The form was for a records request. The records arrived eleven days later. The cover letter said processing took three to five business days. Okonkwo counted: eleven days contained seven business days. She noted this. The records were 340 pages. She had requested twelve. The extra 328 pages belonged to someone with a similar name. She could tell because the other person's name was on every page. She called the number on the cover letter. The number connected to a voicemail. The voicemail said to use the online form. She used the online form. The online form asked for her case number. She did not have a case number. There was no field for explaining this.

The committee had seven members. A quorum required five. Three attended. The meeting was called to order anyway. This was noted in the minutes as "quorum achieved." Nakamura asked how. The answer was not recorded in the minutes. The committee was reviewing a proposal. The proposal was forty-one pages. The executive summary was two pages. It summarized a different proposal. Nobody mentioned this. The committee voted. The vote was two in favor, one abstaining. The abstention was Nakamura. She had not been given the proposal in advance. She had been given a document called "Background Materials." The background materials contained the agenda from a previous meeting. The vote passed. A two-to-nothing vote was required for passage. The minutes recorded it as unanimous. Nakamura was listed as voting in favor. She was not consulted about this. The proposal was forwarded to the subcommittee. The subcommittee had been dissolved in March. The minutes did not reflect this.
rationales: Outputs 1 and 2 share the same casual, informal register, use nearly identical phrasing ('that's the whole point' and the 'own voice is their own voice' construction), and both feature short, punchy sentence structures with minimal elaboration. These are strong stylistic fingerprints pointing to the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same paraphraser. Both use a parallel two-sentence structure, open with a temporal marker ('So far'/'Thus far'), employ formal vocabulary ('uncommon words'/'unusual vocabulary'), and describe the substitution of 'efficacious' with 'effective' as a revealing 'sign'/'signature.' Their tone, sentence rhythm, and rhetorical approach are nearly identical, distinguishing them from the archaic style of 1, the more casual/explanatory style of 2, and the simpler/shorter style of 3.

Outputs 0 and 2 share an almost identical structural template: they both open with three short imperative fragments ('Stop/Pause. No. Hold on.'), contain a long embedded-clause sentence with very similar phrasing about syntactic span and grammar complexity, and close with two ultra-short sentences ('Brief again/Short again. Finished.'). The parallelism is too precise to be coincidental — only minor word substitutions differ (e.g., 'Stop' vs 'Pause', 'Brief' vs 'Short', 'embedded' vs 'nested'), which is characteristic of the same paraphraser producing two slightly varied outputs.

Outputs 0 and 2 share the most similar stylistic fingerprints: both are written as coherent explanatory paragraphs with a formal, moderate tone; both list the synonym words in a flowing narrative rather than bullet points; both frame the concept similarly (repetition as something challenging, machines substituting words); and both end with a comment about nine synonyms being chosen deliberately/skillfully. This contrasts with output 1's extreme 'however' repetition pattern, output 3's very short punchy sentences, and output 4's single analytical paragraph with a different conclusion.

Indexes 1 and 3 both produce identical meta-responses: they claim no text was provided and request input while promising to avoid forbidden words. Neither actually paraphrases the source content. This shared failure pattern and nearly identical template structure (acknowledge missing text + promise to avoid forbidden words + request input) is a clear stylistic fingerprint of the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share very similar stylistic patterns: both use the distinctive phrase 'The machine cannot resist the triple/tricolon,' both maintain concise punchy sentences closely following the source, both include imperative instructions ('Write down some pairs and count what comes back' vs 'Generate pairs. Count the results.'), and both use 'triads/threes' terminology. The structural similarity and shared unique phrasing strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same distinctive stylistic fingerprint: both use fragmented short sentences for the narrative portion, both include nearly identical meta-commentary ('no topic sentence,' 'no as a result,' 'no consequently,' 'The logic is/remains implicit,' 'A paraphraser/rewrite would add framing'), and both conclude with the 'fingerprint' concept. The parallel structure and near-identical phrasing of the analytical commentary strongly indicate the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 3 and 4 both begin with the preamble 'Here's a rewritten version of the text' followed by a colon, which is a distinctive fingerprint. Both adopt a verbose, explanatory prose style, both use 'octopus camouflage,' and both include meta-commentary about the non-linear narrative structure being a deliberate choice. These stylistic patterns consistently match and suggest the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use short, clean declarative sentences where each observation is broken into its own sentence. Both maintain a similar formal-neutral register. Output 1 says 'Marcus recorded it' and Output 3 says 'Marcus recorded this incident' - very parallel phrasing. Both end with a similar conclusion about persistence despite lack of meaning ('Yet he kept writing' vs 'he persisted in his writing'). The sentence-by-sentence breakdown of events is structurally identical in both, distinguishing them from the run-on style of 0 and 4, and the flowery narrative of 2.

Indexes 0 and 4 share the same paraphraser fingerprint: both use full narrative summarizing prose, both add interpretive language ('subtle cues'/'unspoken cues', 'cancelled/deferred meetings'), both describe the team's dissent similarly, both end with the narrative framing about failure/success, and both follow the same structural organization. They differ from indexes 1 and 2 (more detailed/flowing) and index 3 (minimalist fragments), consistently showing a summarizing, explanatory paraphrasing style.

Versions 2 and 4 share the same minimalist, Hemingway-esque style with very short declarative sentences and sparse prose. Both use fragments like 'No one escalated' and maintain a terse, stripped-down narrative. Version 2: 'The files sat in the inbox from February until June. No one escalated.' Version 4: 'The files remained in the inbox from February to June. No one escalated the matter.' Both end with similarly clipped, staccato observations about Henderson not being told and never asking. Their sentence rhythm, fragmented structure, and deliberate minimalism distinguish them from versions 0 (more elaborate prose), 1 (structured but wordy), and 3 (semicolon-heavy run-ons).

Outputs 1 and 3 share very similar stylistic patterns: both use numerals for larger numbers (40, 11, 17) while spelling out 'four,' both use 'set aside' to describe the tabled item, both describe Priya as never having the opportunity to open her laptop, both end by noting the presentation remains in downloads and the funding issue is unresolved, and both employ flowing, explanatory prose rather than fragmented or telegraphic sentences.

Indexes 0 and 2 both preserve the original text's fragmented, sparse literary style with short sentences and minimal editorial intervention. Both maintain specific details like 'four people had forgotten,' 'Nobody knew who Management was,' and the final 'She did not say so/She didn't say so.' Both avoid adding interpretive framing or combining sentences significantly. The structural and stylistic fingerprint of keeping the original's minimalist, punchy sentence rhythm is consistent across both outputs, distinguishing them from indexes 1 (more compressed/editorial), 3 (verbose/interpretive), and 4 (semicolon-heavy run-on style).

Outputs 3 and 4 share very similar stylistic fingerprints: both use clean, direct prose with precise factual presentation, similar sentence structures, and notably both end with the same dual-possibility framing ('noticed and chose to remain silent' vs 'noticed and said nothing—both possibilities'). They also both present the same details in similar order and use 'Final_FINAL_v3' consistently. The tone is measured and neutral throughout both, contrasting with output 1's elaborate style, output 2's stream-of-consciousness semicolon style, and output 0's vagueness.

Outputs 1 and 4 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use 'convene,' both explicitly mention the April minutes recording 'quorum present,' both are written in polished, flowing prose with complete sentences and clear transitions, and both maintain a similar moderate length and professional tone. Output 2 is more verbose with editorial commentary, Output 3 uses heavy semicolon-chaining in a terse stream-of-consciousness style, and Output 0 uniquely uses 'assemble' instead of 'convene' with a more fragmented style.

Indexes 2 and 3 share the most distinctive stylistic and structural similarities: both use spelled-out numbers ('twenty-three,' 'eleven'), both describe the discrepancy going 'unnoticed,' both include seven bullet points starting with 'Employees indicated,' both specifically mention Cressida considering adding an elaboration field but 'ultimately' not doing so, and both conclude with the all-hands meeting and the unaddressed 39%. The paragraph organization and formal, measured tone are nearly identical between these two outputs.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical structure, tone, and content sequencing. Both use short declarative sentences, maintain the same narrative flow, and both end with a meta-commentary about no follow-up question being asked. Output 0 ends 'This was not true, as there was no follow-up question from the manager' while output 4 ends 'This was not true. Her manager did not ask a follow-up question; there was no follow-up question.' — the same rhetorical move of emphasizing the absent follow-up. Minor lexical differences (e.g., 'slide deck' vs. 'slides', 'forty-seven' spelled out vs. '47') are consistent with slight variation from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 4 share similar paraphrasing patterns: both use more narrative/explanatory prose, both paraphrase the third action item as verifying/confirming 'all relevant parties were in agreement' (unlike others who flag it as not a real action item), both add interpretive framing around Petrov's situation, and both use softer, more elaborate sentence structures compared to the terse style of output 2 or the structured enumeration of output 0.

Outputs 2 and 4 share the same distinctive structural choice: both move the signature/name-typing resolution to the END of the passage (after the records story), rather than keeping it at the beginning as in the source. Both also use a minimalist, staccato sentence style with short declarative sentences. Output 4 uses semicolons to chain clauses while Output 2 uses periods, but both are clearly from the same paraphraser given the identical structural reordering and terse sentence rhythm.

Outputs 1 and 3 share the same terse, clipped, minimalist writing style. Both use very short sentences and semicolons to connect clauses, strip out most transitional phrases, omit some details (like the quorum question from Nakamura), and present information in a sparse journalistic shorthand. The sentence constructions are strikingly similar: 'The proposal was forty-one pages; the executive summary was two pages...' (Output 1) vs 'The proposal was forty-one pages long. The executive summary was two pages, and summarized another proposal...' (Output 3). Both avoid the formal paragraph structuring seen in other outputs and share the same minimalist fingerprint.
